title: Tropical Geometry as a Restricted Architecture for Physics-Informed Neural Networks: Applications in Nonlinear Fluid-Structure Examples
authors: Carla Valencia-Negrete, Cristhian Garay-Lopez, Marco Favela-Rodriguez, Alonso Andapia-Viveros
abstract: Nonlinear algebraic (polynomial) differential equations that govern fluid-structure interactions, such as those modeling vortex-induced vibrations, and shock waves, often lack analytical solutions, creating significant challenges to efficient prediction and control. While Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a mesh-free numerical alternative, they frequently suffer from convergence stagnation when optimizing over chaotic landscapes or stiff singularities. This paper introduces a hybrid methodology that integrates tropical differential algebraic geometry with deep learning. Using tropical algebra, we algorithmically determine a hard constraint, which we use to restrict the neural network's hypothesis space to the exact support of the valid formal power series solution. We establish a theoretical Valuation-Support equivalence between classical Briot-Bouquet indicial analysis and the fundamental theorem of tropical differential algebraic geometry, proving that tropical methods accurately identify singularity structures. Numerical experiments on the Van der Pol and Burgers' equations demonstrate that embedding these tropical constraints directly into the network architecture drastically reduces the search space, overcoming optimization stagnation and improving both accuracy and convergence speed in non-homogeneous physical regimes.

infographic.key_insight: The paper shows that tropical differential algebra can act as a hard architectural constraint for PINNs, restricting the network to the correct formal power-series support near singularities.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This can improve simulation and control of nonlinear fluid-structure and shock-driven systems by making PINNs more stable, faster to converge, and less likely to stall on stiff or chaotic problems.
method: Use tropical algebra to infer the valid support of the local solution series.
Embed that support as a hard constraint inside the neural network architecture.
Prove a valuation-support equivalence linking Briot-Bouquet analysis with tropical differential geometry.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments on the Van der Pol and Burgers' equations, showing improved accuracy and convergence speed versus unconstrained PINN optimization.
infographic.watch_item: The approach may depend on identifying the correct local singular structure in advance, which could limit portability to problems where the tropical constraint is hard to derive.
infographic.paper_title: Tropical Geometry as a Restricted Architecture for Physics-Informed Neural Networks: Applications in Nonlinear Fluid-Structure Examples

title: GAIA: Geometry-Adaptive Operator Learning for Forward and Inverse Problems
authors: Meenakshi Krishnan, Pranav Pulijala, Ke Chen, Haizhao Yang, Ramani Duraiswami
abstract: Operator learning for partial differential equations (PDEs) on arbitrary geometries builds fast neural surrogates for large-scale simulation. Although recent geometry-adaptive neural operators have made substantial progress, they are mainly designed for forward problems in which inputs and outputs share the same spatial domain. This limits their applicability for boundary value problems (BVPs) and inverse problems, where inputs and outputs may live on different domains. We introduce the Geometry-Adaptive Integral Autoencoder (GAIA), an operator learning model that encodes the domain boundary and the interior field distribution into geometry tokens, and conditions integral transform layers on these tokens via cross-attention, allowing the kernel to adapt locally to geometric features. This yields a single architecture for forward (including BVPs) and inverse problems on arbitrary domains in one pass, without retraining, iterative optimization, or graph construction. We evaluate GAIA on seven 2D and 3D benchmarks, four of which are new or substantially extended benchmarks for inverse problems and BVP: electrical impedance tomography, optical tomography, 3D Darcy flow on varying geometries, and a modified setting of Poisson BVP on mechanical components benchmark (MCB). GAIA sets new state-of-the-art results on every inverse and BVP task, reducing median relative $L^2$ error by 64% on airfoil flow reconstruction and 27% on EIT relative to the next best amortized method, and outperforming all baselines on every shape category of MCB. On other forward problems, GAIA is competitive with specialized solvers while maintaining stable accuracy across point resolutions on which transformer-based baselines degrade.

infographic.key_insight: GAIA learns geometry-aware operator mappings by turning boundaries and interior fields into tokens, then using cross-attention to adapt integral kernels so one model can solve forward, boundary value, and inverse PDE problems on arbitrary domains.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This could reduce the need for task-specific solvers, retraining, and mesh/graph engineering in scientific simulation workflows across complex geometries.
method: Encodes domain geometry and field data into a shared set of geometry tokens.
Conditions integral transform layers on these tokens with cross-attention for local geometric adaptation.
Uses one architecture for forward, BVP, and inverse problems without iterative optimization or graph construction.
infographic.evidence_signal: Evaluated on seven 2D/3D benchmarks, including four new or extended inverse/BVP tasks, and reported state-of-the-art results on every inverse and BVP case plus strong forward-problem performance.
infographic.watch_item: Performance gains depend on reliable geometry tokenization and may still need careful handling of resolution changes or domain-specific benchmark shifts in deployment.
infographic.paper_title: GAIA: Geometry-Adaptive Operator Learning for Forward and Inverse Problems

title: Formalized $q$-series: The Rogers-Ramanujan Identities and Beyond
authors: Kenny Lau, Seewoo Lee, Ken Ono
abstract: The theory of $q$-series and basic hypergeometric series plays a crucial role at the intersection of combinatorics, number theory, and representation theory. From the classical partition identities of Euler and Jacobi to modern developments in class field theory, vertex operator algebras, and the Monstrous Moonshine conjecture, $q$-series provide the analytic framework for a wide range of profound applications. In this paper, we discuss the formalization of this theory in the Lean proof assistant, a process that requires careful design of scalable and versatile structures to reconcile formal algebraic identities with analytic convergence properties. We address these foundational challenges by focusing on the construction of $q$-Pochhammer symbols, $q$-binomial coefficients, Bailey's Lemma and similar primitives. To demonstrate the utility of this work, we provide fully verified proofs of the Jacobi Triple Product formula and the celebrated Rogers-Ramanujan identities, which serve as both historical and technical benchmarks for the field. This work establishes a rigorous computational foundation for the future formalization of mock theta functions, modular forms, and the diverse algebraic structures that underpin their applications across mathematics and physics.

infographic.key_insight: The paper formalizes core $q$-series machinery in Lean and uses it to verify classical identities like the Jacobi Triple Product and Rogers-Ramanujan identities.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It provides a reusable proof-assistant foundation for scalable formal verification of advanced combinatorics, number theory, and modular-form mathematics.
method: Built Lean formalizations of $q$-Pochhammer symbols and $q$-binomial coefficients
Developed foundational primitives such as Bailey's Lemma
Verified benchmark theorems including the Jacobi Triple Product and Rogers-Ramanujan identities
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract highlights fully verified proofs of the Jacobi Triple Product formula and the Rogers-Ramanujan identities as validation of the formalization approach.
infographic.watch_item: The main implementation challenge is reconciling formal algebraic identities with analytic convergence requirements in a scalable way.
infographic.paper_title: Formalized $q$-series: The Rogers-Ramanujan Identities and Beyond

title: Is One Layer Enough? Training A Single Transformer Layer Can Match Full-Parameter RL Training
authors: Zijian Zhang, Rizhen Hu, Athanasios Glentis, Dawei Li, Chung-Yiu Yau, Hongzhou Lin
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a central component of post-training large language models (LLMs), yet little is understood about how RL adaptation is distributed across transformer layers. Existing approaches typically update all model parameters uniformly, implicitly assuming that every layer contributes similarly to the gains obtained during RL post-training. In this work, we challenge this assumption through a systematic layer-wise study of RL training. Surprisingly, we find that training a single transformer layer can recover most of the gains achieved by full-parameter RL training, and in some cases even surpass it. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the quantity layer contribution, which measures the fraction of full RL improvement recovered by training a layer in isolation. Across seven models spanning two model families (Qwen3, Qwen2.5), three RL algorithms (GRPO, GiGPO, Dr. GRPO), and multiple task domains including mathematical reasoning, code generation, and agentic decision-making, we observe a remarkably stable pattern: RL gains are highly concentrated in a small subset of, and in many cases even a single, transformer layers. More strikingly, the same structural pattern consistently emerges: high-contribution layers concentrate in the middle of the transformer stack, while layers near the input and output ends contribute substantially less. The resulting layer rankings remain strongly correlated across datasets, tasks, model families, and RL algorithms.

infographic.key_insight: RL post-training gains are highly concentrated in a small number of transformer layers, often just one middle layer, which can match or even exceed full-parameter training.
infographic.engineering_relevance: Instead of updating every parameter, practitioners may target a few high-contribution layers to reduce training cost while preserving most RL performance gains.
method: Train individual transformer layers in isolation during RL post-training.
Measure "layer contribution" as the fraction of full-RL improvement recovered by a single layer.
Compare layer rankings across models, tasks, and RL algorithms to test stability.
infographic.evidence_signal: The study evaluates seven models across Qwen3 and Qwen2.5 with GRPO, GiGPO, and Dr. GRPO on math reasoning, code generation, and agentic decision-making tasks.
infographic.watch_item: Layer importance appears task- and model-dependent enough that the best layer should be re-validated before deployment, rather than assuming one fixed layer always works.
infographic.paper_title: Is One Layer Enough? Training A Single Transformer Layer Can Match Full-Parameter RL Training

title: Geometric Signatures of Reasoning: A Spectral Perspective on Task Hardness
authors: Aria Masoomi, Mahsa Bazzaz, Adel Javanmard, Vahab Mirrokni
abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning enables large language models (LLMs) to solve complex problems by generating intermediate reasoning steps. While much attention has been paid to the length and content of these reasoning chains, far less is known about their internal geometry. We study the \emph{geometry} of CoT trajectories in the hidden state space of transformer models, formalizing each reasoning chain as a discrete curve in $\mathbb{R}^d$ and characterizing it through spectral, positional, and kinematic geometric functionals. We introduce the effective dimension $d_ρ$ as a measure of trajectory complexity and show theoretically that trajectories with flatter eigenvalue spectra correspond to harder tasks, as they explore more of the hidden dimensions. Lastly, we explore how kinematic features of the trajectory, mean position, positional dispersion, initial and current hidden states, mean velocity, mean speed, and speed dispersion, can be used to predict solution correctness before generation is complete, and may inform future early-stopping strategies. Experimentally, on mathematical reasoning problems from the MATH500 dataset, $d_ρ$ achieves $0.93$ AUC in distinguishing easy from hard problems, while kinematic features potentially can predict correctness from only the first $20\%$ of generated tokens. These correctness signatures transfer across questions of varying difficulty, establishing that the shape of a model's internal reasoning trajectory is a principled window into both task hardness and solution quality.

infographic.key_insight: CoT reasoning traces form geometric trajectories in hidden-state space, and flatter spectral signatures indicate harder math problems and potentially lower solution quality.
infographic.engineering_relevance: These geometry-based signals could enable early difficulty assessment and early-stopping or routing strategies for LLM reasoning systems.
method: Model each reasoning chain as a discrete curve in hidden-state space.
Compute spectral, positional, and kinematic trajectory features, including effective dimension dρ.
Use early trajectory statistics to predict task hardness and correctness before generation finishes.
infographic.evidence_signal: On MATH500, effective dimension dρ reached 0.93 AUC for easy-vs-hard discrimination, and early kinematic features predicted correctness from the first 20% of generated tokens.
infographic.watch_item: These signals are promising but depend on access to internal hidden states and may require careful validation across models, tasks, and decoding settings.
infographic.paper_title: Geometric Signatures of Reasoning: A Spectral Perspective on Task Hardness

title: Vehicle Routing Problem Meets Large Language Models: An Overview and Perspectives
authors: Xianchao Xiu, Chong Shen, Yanjiao Zhu, Wanquan Liu
abstract: The vehicle routing problem (VRP) is a central optimization problem in artificial intelligence, logistics automation, transportation scheduling, and industrial decision-making. VRP and its variants are NP-hard, and practical routing tasks often combine time windows, vehicle capacities, pickup-and-delivery relations, dynamic requests, and other operational constraints, making both modeling and solving difficult. Large language models (LLMs) provide a flexible interface for routing optimization by processing natural-language requirements, generating code, reasoning over constraints, and interacting with external tools. This survey reviews LLM-driven research on VRP, covering the basic definition, main variants, major solver families, and LLM concepts needed for this topic. Existing studies are organized into three roles: modelers translate natural-language requirements into constraints and modeling code; designers generate heuristics, operators, or route plans; and coordinators organize tool calls, multi-agent collaboration, and connections with neural solvers. The survey also reviews standard benchmarks, real or near-real operational datasets, LLM-oriented evaluation frameworks, and two comparative experiments. The goal is to clarify current progress in LLM-assisted routing optimization and provide a structured reference for intelligent decision-making, advanced manufacturing, and industrial automation.

infographic.key_insight: This survey frames large language models as flexible intermediaries for vehicle routing problems, enabling natural-language modeling, heuristic generation, and tool-driven coordination across VRP variants.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The work is relevant for logistics, transportation scheduling, and industrial automation because it suggests how LLMs can help translate real operational constraints into solvable routing workflows.
method: Translate natural-language routing requirements into constraints and modeling code
Generate heuristics, operators, or full route plans for VRP variants
Coordinate tool calls, multi-agent workflows, and neural solver integration
infographic.evidence_signal: The survey grounds its discussion with standard benchmarks, real or near-real operational datasets, LLM-oriented evaluation frameworks, and two comparative experiments.
infographic.watch_item: LLM outputs for routing are still sensitive to constraint ambiguity and tool integration errors, so generated plans should be checked against formal optimization requirements.
infographic.paper_title: Vehicle Routing Problem Meets Large Language Models: An Overview and Perspectives

title: Beyond the Library: An Agentic Framework for Autoformalizing Research Mathematics
authors: Arshia Soltani Moakhar, Iman Gholami, Max Springer, Mahdi JafariRaviz, MohammadTaghi Hajiaghayi
abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in mathematical reasoning, they frequently produce subtle errors that evade human detection. Formal mathematical languages like Lean 4 offer mechanical proof checking, strongly motivating the need for autoformalization: the automatic translation of natural language mathematics into verifiable code. Recent trends indicate that general-purpose LLMs, heavily optimized for standard programming, now outperform smaller models explicitly fine-tuned for Lean. Leveraging this shift, we introduce an agentic autoformalization framework powered by general coding LLMs. At the core of our system is an orchestrator that manages a multi-agent pipeline tailored for research-level mathematics. Because cutting-edge research frequently relies on concepts outside the scope of existing libraries like Mathlib, our system dynamically extends necessary type definitions and validates them via a novel Auxiliary Lemma technique before formalizing the primary theorems. We applied our approach to PutnamBench, producing machine-checked Lean proofs for a random sample of 32 problems. Furthermore, we evaluate our system on five papers from the ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing (STOC) spanning combinatorics, communication complexity, mechanism design, and learning theory, successfully formalizing their main theorems and validating the generated formalizations with human experts; for all five we also formalize the proofs alongside the statements, and notably two of them are proved with no axioms beyond Lean's kernel. All of our formalizations are available at https://beyondthelibrary.github.io/formal_arxiv .

infographic.key_insight: An agentic multi-LLM pipeline can turn research-level natural language mathematics into machine-checked Lean 4 formalizations, even when the needed concepts are not already in Mathlib.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This points to a practical path for automating theorem formalization in computational science workflows, reducing manual proof engineering while preserving formal verification.
method: Orchestrator coordinates multiple specialized agents for decomposition, translation, and verification.
Dynamically introduces missing type definitions and checks them with an Auxiliary Lemma technique before proving main theorems.
Uses general coding-focused LLMs rather than Lean-specific fine-tuning to exploit stronger programming能力.
infographic.evidence_signal: The system produced machine-checked Lean proofs for 32 randomly sampled PutnamBench problems and successfully formalized and validated the main theorems of five STOC papers, with proofs for all five and two requiring no axioms beyond Lean's kernel.
infographic.watch_item: Results are promising but depend on careful human validation and on correctly extending the formal library, which may be brittle for broader or more novel mathematical domains.
infographic.paper_title: Beyond the Library: An Agentic Framework for Autoformalizing Research Mathematics

title: Structured Factorization Approaches for Quantum State Tomography
authors: Zhen Qin, Joseph M. Lukens, Brian T. Kirby, Zhihui Zhu
abstract: Since the complexity of quantum state tomography (QST) scales exponentially with system size, exploiting priors such as low-rankness, tensor-network structures, and neural-network representations is essential for scalable QST in terms of sample complexity and parameter complexity. In this paper, we introduce a unified framework, termed structured factorization, that builds on BurerMonteiro-type factorization by parametrizing the density matrix as $FF^\dagger$, where the factor $F$ is constrained to belong to a structured model class. This factorization guarantees physical validity by construction while allowing a broad range of structural priors to be incorporated directly through the choice of the factor space, ranging from the generic Cholesky decomposition to low-rank matrices, matrix product operators, and neural density operators based on multilayer perceptron and transformer architectures. Building on this structured factorization framework, we formulate QST as an optimization problem over the factor space from measurement data. We first develop a unified statistical analysis of the sample complexity of least-squares estimation for a broad class of structured quantum states. We then propose a projected gradient descent method that operates directly on the factor space and accommodates a wide range of structural parametrizations and reconstruction objectives. To further exploit the geometry of the maximum-likelihood estimation formulation and the constraints on the factors, we derive a power method that yields a step-size-free algorithm with fast convergence, recovering Covers method as a special case when the factor is unconstrained.

infographic.key_insight: A single factorized form, \u03c1 = FF\u2020, can encode many physically valid quantum-state models while unifying low-rank, tensor-network, and neural priors for tomography.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives a scalable route to quantum state reconstruction that reduces parameter/sample burden and can be adapted to different hardware, data regimes, and model assumptions.
method: Represent the density matrix as FF\u2020 with F chosen from a structured model class.
Formulate QST as optimization over the factor space using least-squares or maximum-likelihood objectives.
Use projected gradient descent or a geometry-aware power method for efficient reconstruction; the latter is step-size-free.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a unified statistical analysis of least-squares sample complexity and proposes optimization algorithms validated across a broad class of structured quantum states.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on choosing an appropriate factor structure; mismatched priors or nonconvex optimization can make reconstruction sensitive to initialization and constraint handling.
infographic.paper_title: Structured Factorization Approaches for Quantum State Tomography

title: Theoria: Rewrite-Acceptability Verification over Informal Reasoning States
authors: Michael Saldivar, Ben Slivinski
abstract: When should an AI system's answer be trusted? Formal proof assistants offer certainty but cannot reach most of the problem distribution; scalar LLM judges offer coverage but produce opaque scores that cannot be audited after the fact and are subject to the same coherence issues as any LLM. We present Theoria, a verification architecture that closes this gap. A candidate solution is rewritten into a sequence of typed state transitions, each licensed by an explicit justification, whether that be a citation, computation, or problem-given fact, and every transition is independently auditable. The foundational invariant is completeness of change: every difference between consecutive proof states must be accounted for, so hidden premises surface as unlicensed mutations rather than passing silently. On HLE-Verified Gold (185 text-only expert problems), Theoria certifies 105 at 91.4% strict precision (Wilson 95% CI [84.5%, 95.4%]). Every certification produces a human readable proof trace in which each step can be independently challenged. Holistic LLM judges achieve comparable precision at matched coverage but fail on different problems (Jaccard 0.14-0.36), making the approaches complementary. On 95 adversarial poisoned proofs across 15 domains, structured judges catch 94.7% versus 83.2% for holistic judging (p= 0.0017). The overall 11.5 pp gap concentrates in hidden premises (90.6% vs. 62.5%, a 28 pp difference) and fabricated citations (100% vs. 90%), the error classes where the formal analysis predicts an advantage; performance is identical on arithmetic and theorem-misapplication errors, where no advantage is predicted. On GPQA Diamond (n= 65), certified precision is 97.1% (Wilson CI [85.1%, 99.5%]).

infographic.key_insight: Theoria verifies AI answers by rewriting them into auditable typed state transitions, so any unsupported change is exposed as an explicit unlicensed mutation.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This makes high-coverage LLM judging more trustworthy for deployment in theorem proving and expert QA by adding post-hoc auditability without relying on full formal proofs.
method: Rewrite candidate answers into a sequence of typed proof-state transitions.
Require each transition to be justified by a citation, computation, or problem-given fact.
Enforce completeness of change so every state difference must be accounted for.
infographic.evidence_signal: On HLE-Verified Gold, Theoria certifies 105/185 problems at 91.4% strict precision, reaches 97.1% certified precision on GPQA Diamond, and catches 94.7% of adversarial poisoned proofs versus 83.2% for holistic judging.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on high-quality typed rewrites and explicit justifications, so weak parsing, incomplete evidence, or ambiguous state representations could reduce coverage or introduce verifier bottlenecks.
infographic.paper_title: Theoria: Rewrite-Acceptability Verification over Informal Reasoning States

title: An Optimisation Framework for the Well-Conditioned Training of Physics-Informed Neural Networks
authors: Joseph Webb, Sadok Jerad, Coralia Cartis
abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have emerged as a promising route to solve partial differential equations, yet they have struggled to reach the precision of classical solvers. The obstacle is increasingly understood to be one of optimisation, owing to the severely ill-conditioned loss landscape. We present $\textbf{DSGNAR}$: Doubly-Sketched Gauss-Newton with Adaptive Ratio, a scalable second-order optimisation framework that confronts this ill-conditioning and, in doing so, obtains unprecedented accuracy and speed. $\textbf{DSGNAR}$ couples a doubly-sketched Gauss-Newton model with a novel strategy that carefully controls both regularisation and step length. Across a suite of problems spanning nonlinear, chaotic, multi-scale, high-dimensional, and Navier-Stokes, the framework greatly improves on the state of the art: able to attain relative $\ell_2$ errors as low as $3\times10^{-16}$ in double precision, improve contemporary results by five orders of magnitude on the canonical Burgers' equation, and as much as eight orders on a high-dimensional Poisson problem, while remaining markedly faster. We further show that, in single precision, solutions at the limit of round-off error can be obtained very quickly: Burgers' equation to $\ell_2^{\text{rel}} = 4.75 \times 10^{-7}$ in under ten seconds. The framework is also robust to the choice of architecture, arithmetic precision, and initial hyperparameters. The code is available at https://www.github.com/wephy/physics-informed-neural-networks

infographic.key_insight: DSGNAR is a second-order training framework for PINNs that directly tackles the core problem of ill-conditioned optimization, enabling much higher accuracy and faster convergence than standard methods.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It makes PINNs more viable for practical PDE solving by delivering near-round-off solutions on challenging equations and scaling better across nonlinear, chaotic, multi-scale, and high-dimensional problems.
method: Uses a doubly-sketched Gauss-Newton approximation to make second-order optimization scalable.
Adds adaptive control of both regularization and step length for stable progress.
Targets improved conditioning of the PINN loss landscape rather than only tuning architecture or learning rates.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports gains across a benchmark suite, including relative L2 errors as low as 3×10^-16 in double precision, five orders of magnitude improvement on Burgers’ equation, and an eight-order improvement on a high-dimensional Poisson problem.
infographic.watch_item: The method depends on careful optimization and numerical precision choices, so performance may vary with problem setup, architecture, and floating-point limits.
infographic.paper_title: An Optimisation Framework for the Well-Conditioned Training of Physics-Informed Neural Networks

title: Geometry-Preserving Orthonormal Initialization for Low-Rank Adaptation in RLVR
authors: Ruijia Zhang, Jiacheng Zhu, Hanqing Zhu, Laixi Shi
abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) and its variants enable parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models under the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) paradigm. However, their efficacy and behavior under Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) are less well understood. In particular, two structurally initialized LoRA variants, PiSSA and MiLoRA, which outperform standard LoRA under SFT, can underperform standard LoRA under RLVR and may even exhibit training instability. These observations suggest that how to initialize the low-rank matrices in RLVR remains unclear. In this work, we develop a theoretical analysis of LoRA in RLVR, showing that orthonormal initialization achieves the minimal gap between LoRA outcome and that of full fine-tuning. Guided by this insight, we propose geometry-preserving orthonormal initialization for low-rank adaptation in RLVR, leading to two new variants, RLPO and RLMO. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that the proposed orthonormal initialization stabilizes RLVR training and outperforms standard LoRA, contrasting with PiSSA and MiLoRA. Finally, our unified analysis for LoRA initialization also explains why PiSSA and MiLoRA can underperform in RLVR, which may be of independent interest. Code and checkpoints are publicly available at https://github.com/Richard-ZZZ/geometry-preserving-orthonormal-init-rlvr.

infographic.key_insight: In RLVR, orthonormal LoRA initialization best preserves the geometry of full fine-tuning, reducing the gap to dense training and improving stability.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For parameter-efficient fine-tuning in math-reasoning RL pipelines, choosing the right low-rank initialization can materially affect convergence, robustness, and final performance.
method: Analyze LoRA dynamics under RLVR and compare initialization schemes to full fine-tuning.
Derive that orthonormal initialization minimizes the outcome gap to full fine-tuning.
Propose geometry-preserving variants RLPO and RLMO based on orthonormal initialization.
infographic.evidence_signal: Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show the proposed initialization stabilizes RLVR training and outperforms standard LoRA, while PiSSA and MiLoRA can underperform.
infographic.watch_item: The results are benchmark-dependent and the benefit may rely on correctly implementing orthonormal initialization within the specific RLVR training setup.
infographic.paper_title: Geometry-Preserving Orthonormal Initialization for Low-Rank Adaptation in RLVR

title: Domain-Decomposed Randomized Neural Networks for Partial Differential Equations in Unbounded Domains
authors: Haixin Wang, Haoning Dang, Fei Wang, Shimin Guo
abstract: Partial differential equations on unbounded domains are challenging because the exterior region must be represented without excessive truncation error. Truncation-based methods often require problem-dependent artificial boundary conditions, while global spectral bases may be inefficient for localized structures, irregular geometries, or solutions with different near-field and far-field behaviors. We propose a domain-decomposed randomized neural network framework for such problems. Different randomized subnetworks are assigned to different spatial regimes: a near-field subnetwork captures local and geometric features, whereas a far-field subnetwork represents exterior decay. The subnetworks are coupled by boundary and interface conditions, and only the output-layer coefficients are solved from linear least-squares systems arising from Petrov--Galerkin or collocation formulations. We develop a Petrov--Galerkin method for semi-unbounded elliptic problems and a collocation method for fully unbounded, perforated, and time-dependent problems. A conditional bounded-parameter approximation result is proved in a broken Sobolev norm, together with an error decomposition covering approximation, empirical-consistency/quadrature, and least-squares optimization errors. Numerical experiments for Poisson and time-dependent Schrödinger equations demonstrate the accuracy and flexibility of the proposed method.

infographic.key_insight: The paper decomposes unbounded PDE domains into near-field and far-field regions, using separate randomized neural subnetworks and linear least-squares fitting to handle local structure and exterior decay efficiently.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a practical surrogate solver for large-scale exterior-domain PDEs where truncation boundaries, irregular geometries, or mixed near/far behavior make standard methods costly or inaccurate.
method: Assign different randomized subnetworks to different spatial regimes (near-field vs. far-field).
Enforce coupling through boundary and interface conditions, then solve only output coefficients by linear least squares.
Use Petrov–Galerkin for semi-unbounded elliptic problems and collocation for fully unbounded, perforated, and time-dependent cases.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments on Poisson and time-dependent Schrödinger equations, along with a proven approximation result and an error decomposition covering approximation, quadrature/consistency, and optimization effects.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on how well the domain split, quadrature, and interface constraints are chosen, since the method’s guarantees are conditional and least-squares accuracy can be sensitive to setup.
infographic.paper_title: Domain-Decomposed Randomized Neural Networks for Partial Differential Equations in Unbounded Domains

title: ZO-Act: Efficient Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning via One-Shot Activation-Informed Low-Rank Subspaces
authors: Xun Dong, Yibo Xu, Naigang Wang, Xin Li, Penghang Yin, Zi Yang
abstract: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization enables fine-tuning large language models when backpropagation is unavailable or memory-prohibitive, but existing methods often perturb full model weights or randomly constructed low-dimensional subspaces, yielding high-variance estimates and limited performance. We propose ZO-Act, an activation-informed ZO fine-tuning method that restricts perturbations to a fixed low-rank subspace derived from input activations. For each linear layer, ZO-Act computes a small activation basis once at initialization and optimizes only lightweight coefficient matrices using forward-only loss evaluations. This reduces the effective perturbation dimension, exposes explicit trainable variables compatible with momentum-based optimizers such as Adam, and naturally supports quantized LLM fine-tuning by keeping low-bit weights frozen. We analyze ZO-Act as zeroth-order optimization over a restricted coefficient space and show that perturbing the low-dimensional coefficients reduces both the variance-dependent convergence term and the finite-difference error of the ZO estimator, at the cost of a controlled subspace approximation bias that is mitigated by the low-rank structure of LLM activations and gradients. Experiments on Llama-3-8B, OPT-13B, and INT4 Llama-3-8B show consistent gains over strong ZO fine-tuning baselines across language understanding, question answering, and commonsense reasoning.

infographic.key_insight: ZO-Act speeds zeroth-order fine-tuning by optimizing only low-rank activation-derived coefficients instead of perturbing full model weights, which lowers estimator variance and makes forward-only training more practical.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The method is especially useful for memory-constrained or backpropagation-free LLM adaptation, including quantized models where weights must remain frozen.
method: Compute a small activation basis once at initialization for each linear layer.
Optimize lightweight coefficient matrices in a fixed low-rank subspace using forward-only loss evaluations.
Use the explicit coefficients with momentum optimizers like Adam while keeping low-bit weights frozen.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports consistent improvements over strong zeroth-order baselines on Llama-3-8B, OPT-13B, and INT4 Llama-3-8B across language understanding, QA, and commonsense reasoning tasks.
infographic.watch_item: Performance still depends on how well the activation-based low-rank subspace captures the true gradient directions, so subspace approximation bias can limit gains in some layers or tasks.
infographic.paper_title: ZO-Act: Efficient Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning via One-Shot Activation-Informed Low-Rank Subspaces

title: Convolutional Symmetric AutoEncoders: enhancing latent stability via differential geometry
authors: G. Li Causi, N. Tonicello, L. Magri, G. Rozza
abstract: Autoencoders (AEs) have emerged as powerful tools for non-linear dimensionality reduction, often surpassing traditional linear methods such as Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) in scenarios characterized by slowly decaying Kolmogorov $n$-widths. In the realm of Reduced-Order Modelling (ROM), these models are increasingly utilized to learn low-dimensional representations of solution manifolds associated with parametric Partial Differential Equations (PDEs). However, the high expressivity of AEs presents a challenge: although trained networks typically minimize reconstruction error, they often struggle to capture the essential properties necessary for building accurate and robust ROMs. Recent works by arXiv:2307.15288v2 and arXiv:2506.11641v1 have tackled this challenge in fully connected AEs by proposing representation-consistent architectures, which preserve some of the properties belonging to POD. This study builds upon that concept by extending representation consistency for convolutional layers. We introduce a novel class of symmetric Convolutional AutoEncoders (CAEs) designed to embody the primary properties of manifold parametrization mappings. When integrated into a ROM framework, this architecture demonstrates significantly improved predictive capabilities. Specifically, we compared the performance of the ROMs based on classical and symmetric CAEs on three one dimensional academic test cases, namely the Linear Advection, the Viscous Burger and the Kuramoto Sivashinsky equation. Numerical results demonstrate that our proposed symmetric approach consistently yields more accurate latent trajectories, lower reconstruction errors, and enhanced model robustness.

infographic.key_insight: The paper shows that enforcing representation consistency in convolutional autoencoders can make the latent space more stable and ROM-ready, not just low-error reconstruction.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This improves reduced-order models for parametric PDEs by producing more reliable latent dynamics and more accurate predictions from compact neural surrogates.
method: Design symmetric convolutional autoencoders that mirror key geometric properties of manifold parametrizations.
Extend representation-consistent ideas from fully connected autoencoders to convolutional layers.
Embed the learned latent space inside a reduced-order modeling pipeline for PDE solution manifolds.
infographic.evidence_signal: The authors test classical vs symmetric CAE-based ROMs on three 1D benchmarks—Linear Advection, Viscous Burgers, and Kuramoto–Sivashinsky—and report consistently better latent trajectories, lower reconstruction error, and improved robustness.
infographic.watch_item: The results are demonstrated on three one-dimensional academic cases, so broader performance on higher-dimensional or strongly nonlinear PDE settings still needs validation.
infographic.paper_title: Convolutional Symmetric AutoEncoders: enhancing latent stability via differential geometry

title: From Finite Cayley Graphs to Growth of Infinite Groups
authors: Tal Weissblat
abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have recently been shown to learn algebraic properties of finite groups from their Cayley graphs [1,2]. In this work, we investigate whether such models generalize to infinite finitely generated groups. Motivated by Gromov's theorem [3], a GNN is trained and validated exclusively on finite complete and truncated Cayley graphs, and then evaluated, without retraining, on truncated Cayley graphs of unseen infinite groups. The evaluation includes free abelian groups of various ranks, the discrete Heisenberg group, the infinite dihedral group, free groups, and direct products with both infinite abelian and finite groups. The results show strong generalization across these families, suggesting that finite Cayley graphs encode sufficient local geometric information to transfer to the infinite setting. Overall, this provides evidence that GNNs trained solely on finite groups can capture geometric features related to the growth of infinite finitely generated groups.

infographic.key_insight: Graph neural networks trained only on finite Cayley graphs can generalize to truncated Cayley graphs of unseen infinite groups, indicating that local graph structure carries growth-related algebraic information.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This suggests a path for using learned graph representations to infer structural properties of large or infinite group-like systems from finite local samples.
method: Train and validate a GNN on finite complete and truncated Cayley graphs.
Evaluate the frozen model on truncated Cayley graphs from unseen infinite groups.
Test across free abelian groups, the discrete Heisenberg group, the infinite dihedral group, free groups, and direct products.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports strong zero-shot generalization from finite-group training data to multiple families of infinite finitely generated groups.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on truncation depth and the extent to which finite local neighborhoods preserve the global growth behavior of the infinite group.
infographic.paper_title: From Finite Cayley Graphs to Growth of Infinite Groups

title: Stabilization Learning: A Paradigm Transition Bridging Control Theory and Machine Learning
authors: Quan Quan
abstract: Stabilization learning is an interdisciplinary paradigm that bridges control theory and machine learning. Its core idea is to enable systems to adjust their policies under perturbations or environmental changes through real-time feedback and adaptive mechanisms. It takes stability as its primary goal, distinguishing itself from certificate learning, which focuses on formal proofs, and reinforcement learning, which pursues optimality. It encompasses a range of methods, including Lyapunov-based analysis and design, deep feature extraction, and data-driven feedback synthesis, and is applicable to complex high-dimensional, nonlinear systems. This paper elaborates on the two major categories of stability in stabilization learning, as well as three typical application scenarios: control, observation, and recognition. It constructs a unified mathematical framework based on a six-tuple, and expands into two types of seven-tuple models: constrained learning with barrier spaces and tracking problems with targets. It also analyzes the roles, meanings, and implementation choices of key elements such as state space, controlled system, metrics, and policy. Through the formal reformulation of 11 types of problems, including multi-agent cooperative tracking, visual servo robot position stabilization, chess games, and Push-T tasks, this paper illustrates the potential applicability of the framework across multiple domains. Finally, it points out that future stabilization learning will focus on two major directions: constructing a unified problem framework and achieving efficient and robust learning, providing solutions for complex system control that combine theoretical rigor with engineering practicality.

infographic.key_insight: Stabilization learning reframes control-and-ML systems around maintaining stability under perturbations, using feedback and adaptive mechanisms rather than solely optimality or formal certification.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The framework is relevant for high-dimensional nonlinear systems where robust real-time control, observation, and recognition must remain stable under changing conditions.
method: Lyapunov-based stability analysis and design
Deep feature extraction and data-driven feedback synthesis
Unified six-tuple / extended seven-tuple formulations for constrained and tracking problems
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a formal reformulation of 11 problem types across control, observation, and recognition to demonstrate broad applicability rather than a single empirical benchmark.
infographic.watch_item: The framework’s practical success may depend on careful choice of state space, metrics, and policy, especially when scaling to complex real-world systems.
infographic.paper_title: Stabilization Learning: A Paradigm Transition Bridging Control Theory and Machine Learning

title: From Answer Generators to Reasoning Facilitators: Designing AI Tutors for Mathematical Reasoning in High-Stakes Environments
authors: Yuming Feng, Yuan Tian, Erica Zhao
abstract: The rapid integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into educational technology threatens to reduce mathematical learning to mere answer generation. This paper presents a generative study, usability study, and 12-participant field deployment of AITutor, an interactive system that translates theoretical pedagogical mechanisms into concrete user interface features. We explore how junior-high students preparing for high-stakes exams (Zhongkao) interact with AI tutoring. Through mixed-methods triangulation (7,379 telemetry events, 8 contextual observations, 10 interviews), we reveal that students actively resist traditional Socratic dialogue under time pressure, repurposing "answer-first" shortcuts as vital diagnostic checkpoints. We demonstrate how features like layered worked examples, step-linked visual grounding, and metacognitive scaffolding lower the interaction cost of reasoning repair. We contribute a "Reasoning-Centered Product Loop," offering actionable implications for designing AI that structurally supports the inspection, local repair, curriculum verification, and delayed retrieval of mathematical reasoning in the wild.

infographic.key_insight: AI tutors for exam prep should facilitate reasoning repair and verification, not just push Socratic dialogue or final answers.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The paper translates pedagogy into interface features such as layered worked examples, step-linked visual grounding, and metacognitive scaffolding that reduce the cost of checking and fixing mathematical reasoning.
method: Generative study to define the tutor design goals
Usability study of AITutor interactions
12-participant field deployment with mixed-methods triangulation
infographic.evidence_signal: The evaluation combined 7,379 telemetry events, 8 contextual observations, and 10 interviews from junior-high students preparing for Zhongkao.
infographic.watch_item: Results may be context-specific to high-stakes exam preparation, so the design may not transfer directly to low-pressure learning settings or other subjects without adaptation.
infographic.paper_title: From Answer Generators to Reasoning Facilitators: Designing AI Tutors for Mathematical Reasoning in High-Stakes Environments

title: The Calibration Turn in AI-Assisted Research: A Conceptual and Methodological Framework for Evidence-Licensed Claims
authors: Hongmin Li
abstract: AI-assisted research has entered a stage in which the central question is not only whether systems can generate hypotheses, run experiments, or produce manuscripts, but whether their scientific claims are calibrated to the evidence that supports them. This Perspective-style paper develops a conceptual and methodological framework for evidence-licensed claims in AI-assisted research. Motivated by representative routes including specialized scientific foundation models, LLM research assistants, multi-agent co-scientists, AI Scientist pipelines, mathematical discovery agents, and self-driving laboratories, it represents AI-assisted research as five operators: hypothesis generation, model-mediated consequence derivation, external validation, belief update, and claim calibration. The central claim is that calibration is not merely cautious wording but a mechanism for managing scientific assertion rights: evidence licenses some forms of speech and withholds others. The paper distinguishes linguistic, consequence-based, interventional, and evidence-licensed semantics; defines the claim-evidence gap and epistemic debt; and treats minimal structural reconstruction across heterogeneous outputs as an upward form of claim calibration. AISim-Cal is included as an illustrative synthetic dynamics exercise, not as an empirical forecast or benchmark. The resulting principles are: no claim without license, validation does not determine claim level, and automation amplifies the need for calibration. Reliable AI-assisted research is therefore evaluated as a loop that generates hypotheses, derives testable consequences, accepts independent adjudication, updates beliefs, and outputs only evidence-licensed claims.

infographic.key_insight: The paper argues that in AI-assisted research, the key problem is not generating outputs but calibrating scientific claims to the evidence that actually licenses them.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For AI research systems, this implies you need explicit claim-level controls that separate hypothesis generation, validation, and final assertion strength.
method: Models AI-assisted research as five operators: hypothesis generation, consequence derivation, external validation, belief update, and claim calibration.
Introduces evidence-licensed semantics plus the ideas of claim-evidence gap and epistemic debt.
Treats minimal structural reconstruction as a way to upward-calibrate claims across heterogeneous AI outputs.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract frames AISim-Cal only as a synthetic dynamics exercise, so the paper is conceptual and methodological rather than an empirical benchmark study.
infographic.watch_item: A practical challenge is enforcing claim calibration across diverse AI pipelines, since validation alone does not guarantee that the final wording is evidence-licensed.
infographic.paper_title: The Calibration Turn in AI-Assisted Research: A Conceptual and Methodological Framework for Evidence-Licensed Claims

title: Decentralized Stochastic Subgradient-type Methods with Communication Compression for Nonsmooth Nonconvex Optimization
authors: Siyuan Zhang, Nachuan Xiao, Xin Liu
abstract: In this paper, we consider the nonsmooth nonconvex decentralized optimization problem, where inter-agent communication is compressed. We propose a general framework that unifies various decentralized stochastic subgradient-type methods with unbiased compression and contractive compression with error compensation. By relating the consensus-error iterates and the averaged iterates to the trajectories of continuous-time differential inclusions, we establish global convergence for all methods encompassed by our framework when the objective functions are nonsmooth and lack Clarke regularity. Based on our framework, we further develop several compression-based methods, including decentralized stochastic subgradient methods utilizing sign-based regularization and gradient-tracking momentum. Preliminary numerical experiments empirically support our theoretical results and highlight the communication-accuracy trade-off of the newly developed methods.

infographic.key_insight: The paper gives a unified convergence framework for decentralized stochastic subgradient methods under compressed communication, even for nonsmooth nonconvex objectives without Clarke regularity.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It informs how to design communication-efficient distributed optimization algorithms for large-scale edge, sensor, and multi-agent systems where bandwidth is limited.
method: Builds a general analysis framework covering unbiased compression and contractive compression with error compensation.
Links consensus-error and averaged-iterate dynamics to continuous-time differential inclusions to prove global convergence.
Develops new compressed decentralized methods using sign-based regularization and gradient-tracking momentum.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports preliminary numerical experiments that empirically validate the theory and illustrate the communication-accuracy trade-off.
infographic.watch_item: The numerical evidence is only preliminary, so practical performance may depend strongly on the compression scheme, step-size choices, and network topology.
infographic.paper_title: Decentralized Stochastic Subgradient-type Methods with Communication Compression for Nonsmooth Nonconvex Optimization

title: Think in English, Answer in Korean: Efficient Adaptation of Multilingual Tool-Using Agents
authors: Utsav Garg, Sungjin Hong, Jason Jung, Justin Lee, Shaan Desai, Joon Hee Kim
abstract: We present LuckyStar 111B, a 111B-parameter hybrid reasoning model developed through a collaboration between Cohere and LG CNS for Korean-English enterprise agents under practical memory and serving constraints. The model trains from Cohere's fully post-trained Command A model rather than a new pretraining run, and uses preamble conditioning to switch between concise non-reasoning behavior and longer tool-oriented reasoning. We study four choices for scaling tool-using agents efficiently: multilingual supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for multi-step tool-use tasks, language-consistency rewards for Korean user-facing responses, and 4-bit quantization for single-GPU serving. The adapted model improves mathematical reasoning, function calling, and agentic natural-language-to-SQL (NL2SQL) performance while preserving general Korean and English instruction-following quality. These results provide a practical recipe and failure-mode analysis for adapting post-trained multilingual models to verifiable agentic workflows under memory-constrained deployment.

infographic.key_insight: A post-trained 111B multilingual model can be efficiently adapted for Korean-English tool use by combining preamble-controlled reasoning with targeted supervised and reinforcement learning.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The work shows a practical path to deploy strong agentic reasoning on constrained hardware using 4-bit quantization and single-GPU serving while keeping Korean and English instruction quality intact.
method: Multilingual supervised fine-tuning for tool-use and instruction following
Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for multi-step tasks and Korean response consistency
4-bit quantization to enable memory-efficient serving
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports improved mathematical reasoning, function calling, and agentic NL2SQL performance, along with preserved general Korean and English instruction-following quality.
infographic.watch_item: Benefits may depend on carefully designed verifiable rewards and preamble control, and quantization could still introduce serving-time accuracy tradeoffs in some workflows.
infographic.paper_title: Think in English, Answer in Korean: Efficient Adaptation of Multilingual Tool-Using Agents
